concepts.title: Structure of Ionic Compounds
concepts.conceptBody:
Structure of Ionic Compounds

Simple ionic compounds are of two types i.e. A B and $\text{A}{{\text{B}}_{2}}$ type. From the knowledge of close packed structures and the voids developed there in, we can have an idea about the structures of simple ionic compounds.

Among the two ions, constituting the binary compounds, the anions usually constitute the space lattice with hcp or ccp type of arrangements whereas the cations, occupy the interstitial voids.

(a) If the anions $\left( {{\text{B}}^{-}} \right)$constitute the crystal lattice and all octahedral voids are occupied by cations $\left( {{\text{A}}^{+}} \right)$, then the formula of the tetrahedral voids are occupied by cations, then the formula of the solid crystal becomes ${{\text{A}}^{+}}{{\text{B}}^{-}}$

(b) When the anions $\left( {{\text{B}}^{-2}} \right)$ are constituting space lattice and all the tetrahedral voids are occupied by the cations $\left( {{\text{A}}^{+}} \right)$, then the formula of the solid crystal will be ${{\text{A}}_{2}}~\text{B}$.

concepts.title: Ionic Compounds of the type AB {Sodium Chloride (Rock salt) type structure}
concepts.conceptBody:
Ionic Compounds of the type AB

Ionic compounds of the type $\text{AB}$ have three types of crystalline structures. (A) $\text{ZnS}$ type (B) $\text{NaCl}$ types (C) $\text{CsCl}$ types

1. Sodium Chloride (Rock salt) type structure

The sodium chloride structure is composed of $\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}$and $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$ions. The number of sodium ions is equal to that of $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$ions. The radii of $\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}$and $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$ions $95\text{pm}$ and $181\text{pm}$ giving the radius ratio of 0.524

$\frac{{{\text{r}}_{\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}}}}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}}}}=\frac{95}{181}=0.524$

The radius ratio of 0.524 for $\text{NaCl}$ suggest an octahedral void. Thus the salient features of this structure are as follows:

(i) Chloride ions (In a typical unit cell) are arranged in cubic close packing (ccp). In this arrangement $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$ions are present at the corners and at the centre of each face of the cube. This arrangement is also regarded as face centred cubic arrangement (fcc).

(ii) The sodium ions are present in all the octahedral holes.

(iii) Since, the number of octahedral holes in ccp structure is equal to the number of anions, every octahedral hole is occupied by $\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}$ions. So that the formula of sodium chloride is $\text{NaCl}$ i.e. stoichiometry of $\text{NaCl}$ is 1: 1.

(iv) Since there are six octahedral holes around each chloride ions, each$\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$ion is surrounded by $6\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}$ions. Similarly each $\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}$ion is surrounded by $6\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$ions. Therefore, the coordination number of $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$as well as of $\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}$ions is six. This is called 6:6 coordination.

(v) It should be noted that $\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}$ions to exactly fit the octahedral holes, the radius ration $\frac{{{\text{r}}_{\text{Na}}}}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}}}}$should be equal to 0.414. However, the actual radius ration $\left( \frac{{{\text{r}}_{\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}}}}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}}}}=0.524 \right)$ exceeds this value. Therefore to accommodate large $\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}$ions, the $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$ions move apart slightly i.e. they do not touch each other and form an expanded face centred lattice.

(vi) The unit cell of sodium chloride has 4 sodium and 4 chloride ions as calculated below

No of sodium ions $=12$ (at edge centres) $1/4+1$ (at body centre) $1=4$

No of chloride ions $=8($ at corner $)\quad 1/8+6($ at face centres $)\quad 1/2=4$

Thus, the number of $\text{NaCl}$ units per unit cell is 4 .

(vii) The edge length of the unit cell of $\text{NaCl}$ type of crystal is $2(r+\text{R})\left( \text{r}= \right.$ radii of $\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}$ion)

$\text{a}=2\left( {{\text{r}}_{\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}}}+{{\text{r}}_{\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}}} \right)\quad \left( \text{R}=\text{ radii of C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}\text{ion } \right)$

Thus, the distance between $\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}$and $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$ions $=\text{a}/2$

Most of the halides of alkali metals, oxides and sulphides of alkaline earth metals have this type of structures. Some of the common examples are $\text{NaI},\text{KCl},\text{Rbl},\text{RbF},\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{Cl},\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{Br}$, $\text{AgCl},\text{AgBr}$ and $\text{AgI}$.

concepts.title: Zinc blende (ZnS) type structures
concepts.conceptBody:
2. Zinc blende (ZnS) type structures (sphelerite)

The zinc sulphide crystals are composed of equal number of $\text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{+2}}$ and ${{\text{S}}^{2-}}$ ions. The radii of the two ions $\left( \text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{+2}}=74\text{pm} \right.$ and $\left. {{\text{S}}^{-2}}=184\text{pm} \right)$ led to the radius $\left( {{\text{r}}^{+}}/{{\text{r}}^{-}} \right)$as 0.40 which suggests a tetrahedral arrangement.

$\frac{{{\text{r}}_{\text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{+2}}}}}{{{\text{r}}_{{{\text{s}}^{-2}}}}}=\frac{74}{184}=0.40$

The salient features of this structure are as follows:

(i) The Zinc ions are arranged in ccp arrangement, i.e. sulphide ions are present at the corners and the centres of each face of the cube

(ii) Zinc ions occupy tetrahedral hole. Only half of the tetrahedral holes are occupied by$\text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{+2}}$ so that the formula of the zinc sulphide is $\text{ZnS}$ i.e. the stoichiometry of the compound is 1: 1(Only alternate tetrahedral holes are occupied by$\text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{+2}}$)

(iii) Since the void is tetrahedral, each zinc ion is surrounded by four sulphide ions and each sulphide ion is surrounded tetrahedrally by four zinc ions. Thus zinc sulphide has$4.4\text{Co}-$ordination.

(iv) For exact fitting of$\text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{+2}}$ in the tetrahedral holes, formed by close packing of ${{\text{S}}^{-2}}$ ions, the ratio $\text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{+2}}/{{\text{S}}^{-2}}$ should be 0.225 . Actually this ratio is slightly large $(0.40)$

(v) There are four $\text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{+2}}$ ions and four ${{\text{S}}^{-2}}$ ions per unit cell as calculated below: No. of ${{\text{S}}^{-2}}$ ions $=8$ (at corners) $\quad 1/8+6$ (at face centres) $\quad 1/2=4$

No. of $\text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{+2}}$ ions $=4$ (within the body) $1=4$ Thus, the number of $\text{ZnS}$ units per unit cell is equal to 4. Some more examples of ionic solids having Zinc blende structures are $\text{CuC}$, $\text{CuBr},\text{CuI},\text{AgI}$, beryllium sulphide.

(vi) Radius Relation: ${{\text{r}}_{\text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{+2}}}}+{{\text{r}}_{{{\text{s}}^{-2}}}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}\text{a}}{4}$ where $\text{a}$ is the edge length.

concepts.conceptBody:

concepts.title: Effect of Temperature on Crystal Structure
concepts.conceptBody:
Effect of Temperature on Crystal Structure

Increase of temperature decreases the coordination of number, e.g. upon heating to $760~\text{K}$, the $\text{CsCl}$ type crystal structure having coordination 8: 8 changed to $\text{NaCl}$ type crystal structures having coordination 6: 6

$\underset{\text{ (8:8 coordination) }}{\mathop{\text{CsCl type crystal }}}\,\xrightarrow{high\,\,temperature}\underset{(6:6\text{ Co-ordination })}{\mathop{\text{NaCl type crystal }}}\,$

concepts.title: Effect of Pressure on Crystal Structure
concepts.conceptBody:
Effect of Pressure on Crystal Structure

Increase of pressure increases the $\text{Co}$ - ordination number during crystallization e.g. by applying pressure, the $\text{NaCl}$ type crystal structure having 6:6 coordination number changes to $\text{CsCl}$ type crystal having coordination number 8: 8

$\underset{\text{ (6:6 coordination) }}{\mathop{\text{NaCl type crystal }}}\,\xrightarrow{high\,\,pressure}\underset{\text{ (8:8 Co-ordination) }}{\mathop{\text{CsCl type crystal }}}\,$

concepts.title: Ionic Compound of the Type AB2{Calcium Fluoride (Fluorite) Structure}
concepts.conceptBody:
Ionic Compound of the Type $\mathbf{A}{{\mathbf{B}}_{\mathbf{2}}}$

Calcium Fluoride (Fluorite) Structure

The salient features of fluorite structure are

(i) The $\text{C}{{\text{a}}^{+2}}$ ions are arranged in ccp arrangement, i.e. these ions occupy all the corners and the centres of each face of the cube.

(ii) The ${{\text{F}}^{-}}$ions all the tetrahedral holes.

(iii) Since there are two tetrahedral holes for each $\text{C}{{\text{a}}^{+2}}$ ion and ${{\text{F}}^{-}}$ions occupy all the tetrahedral holes, there will be two ${{\text{F}}^{-}}$ions for each $\text{C}{{\text{a}}^{+2}}$ ions, thus the stoichiometry of the compound is 1: 2.

(iv) Each$\text{C}{{\text{a}}^{+2}}$ ion is surrounded by $8~{{\text{F}}^{+}}$ions and each ${{\text{F}}^{-}}$ions is surrounded by $4\text{C}{{\text{a}}^{+2}}$ ions. The Coordination number of $\text{C}{{\text{a}}^{+2}}$ ion is eight and that of ${{\text{F}}^{-}}$ion is four, this is called 8:4 Coordination.

(v) Each unit cell has 4 calcium ions and 8 fluoride ions as explained below

No. of $\text{C}{{\text{a}}^{+2}}$ ions $=8$ (at corners) $1/8+6$ (at face centres) $1/2$

No. of $F$ ions $=8$ (within the body) $\quad 1=8$

Thus the number of $\text{Ca}{{\text{F}}_{2}}$ units per unit cell is 4 . Other examples of structure are

$\text{Sr}{{\text{F}}_{2}},\text{BaC}{{\text{l}}_{2}},\text{Ba}{{\text{F}}_{2}},\text{Cd}{{\text{F}}_{2}},\text{Hg}{{\text{F}}_{2}},\text{Cu}{{\text{F}}_{2}},\text{SrC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\text{, etc}\text{. }$

(vi) $\text{ }{{\text{r}}_{\text{C}{{\text{a}}^{+2}}}}+{{\text{r}}_{{{\text{F}}^{-}}}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}\text{a}}{4}\text{ where a is the edge length }$

concepts.title: Ionic Compound of A2B Type
concepts.conceptBody:
Ionic Compound of ${{\mathbf{A}}_{\mathbf{2}}}\mathbf{B}$ Type

The compound having ${{\text{A}}_{2}}~\text{B}$ formula are compounds having anti fluorite structure

Anti fluorite structure is having arrangement of cations and anions opposite to the fluorite structure $\text{L}{{\text{i}}_{2}}\text{O}$ has an anti fluorite structure.

(i) In the crystal structure of $\text{L}{{\text{i}}_{2}}\text{O}$ the ${{\text{O}}^{-2}}$ ions constitute a cubic close packed lattice (fcc structure) and the Li + ions occupy all the tetrahedral voids.

concepts.title: Structure of Fe3O4
concepts.conceptBody:
Structure of $\mathbf{F}{{\mathbf{e}}_{\mathbf{3}}}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{4}}}$ (Magnetite)

In $\text{F}{{\text{e}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}_{4}},\text{F}{{\text{e}}^{+2}}$ and $\text{F}{{\text{e}}^{+3}}$ ions are present in the ratio 2:1. It may be considered as having composition $\text{FeO}.\text{F}{{\text{e}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$. In $\text{F}{{\text{e}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ Oxide arranged in ccp. $\text{F}{{\text{e}}^{+2}}$ ions occupy octahedral voids while $\text{F}{{\text{e}}^{+3}}$ ions are equally distributed between octahedral and tetrahedral voids.

$\text{MgF}{{\text{e}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ also has structure similar to magnetite. In this $\text{M}{{\text{g}}^{+2}}$ ions are present in place of $\text{F}{{\text{e}}^{+2}}$ ion in $\text{F}{{\text{e}}_{3}}\text{O}$. Magnetite has inverse spinel structure.

concepts.title: Summary of various structures of ionic solids
concepts.conceptBody:
Summary of various structures of ionic solids

Example

The unit cell cube length for $\text{LiCl}$ is 5.14{${{\text{d}}_{\text{L}{{\text{i}}^{+}}-\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}}}=\frac{5.14}{2}=2.57{Å}$

${{\text{d}}_{\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}-\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}}}=\sqrt{{{(2.57)}^{2}}+{{(2.57)}^{2}}}\quad =3.63{Å}$

The radius of $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$

$=\frac{{{\text{d}}_{\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}-\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}}}}{2}=\frac{3.63}{2}=1.815{Å}$}$. Assuming anion cation contact, calculate the ionic radius for chloride ion.

Solution: 

The distance between $\text{L}{{\text{i}}^{+}}$ and $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$ion can be derived as half of the edge length of cube.

 

${{\text{d}}_{\text{L}{{\text{i}}^{+}}-\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}}}=\frac{5.14}{2}=2.57{Å}$

${{\text{d}}_{\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}-\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}}}=\sqrt{{{(2.57)}^{2}}+{{(2.57)}^{2}}}\quad =3.63{Å}$

The radius of $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$

$=\frac{{{\text{d}}_{\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}-\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}}}}{2}=\frac{3.63}{2}=1.815{Å}$

concepts.title: Bragg Equation
concepts.conceptBody:
Bragg Equation

We can determine the size of the unit cell and the arrangement of atoms in a crystal from measurement of the diffraction of X-rays by the crystal. When a beam of monochromatic X-ray strikes two planes of atoms in a crystal at a certain angle $\theta $, it is reflected. Intensity of the reflected beam is maximum if

$\text{n}\lambda =2~\text{d}\sin \theta $

This is called Bragg equation where $\text{n}$ is the order of reflection, $\lambda $ the wavelength of X-ray light used, $\text{d}$ the distance between two planes.
